note: Sibling drain loop calls internalLogIf(ctx, scanDir(pop)) with no context.Canceled handling, the exact scanDir error pattern the change converted to return-on-cancel in the parallel pop loop of the same function.

a.reason: Same as rf-087: the drain loop's existing per-iteration contextCanceled check returns ctx.Err() on the very next iteration and logIgnoreError already suppresses context.Canceled from internalLogIf, so the return-on-cancel rewrite adds nothing there, consistent with upstream never propagating it.

b.reason: Same as rf-087: the sibling drain loop's existing per-iteration contextCanceled check and ctx.Done() select already deliver the early-exit behavior the commit added to the inner pop loop, so no functional propagation is needed.

note: Sibling repair_gitfile contains the identical 'if (err == READ_GITFILE_ERR_NOT_A_FILE)' check, and the commit's own first hunk applies the same IS_A_DIR addition at that exact site, proving the change applies.

a.reason: The very same commit's first hunk already adds the READ_GITFILE_ERR_IS_A_DIR case to repair_gitfile's check (hunk @@ -673,7), i.e. the sibling function was updated in the same commit and the flagged block merely ends two lines above the already-fixed site, leaving nothing to propagate.

b.reason: The same commit's first hunk already applies the identical IS_A_DIR addition to repair_gitfile (the `if (err == READ_GITFILE_ERR_NOT_A_FILE)` at line 676); the flagged block (665-674) just ends one line above the patched site, so the sibling was in fact updated, not missed.
